To make it even more complicated, CO2 reduction may proceed via several different reaction pathways with the transfer of 2, 4, 6, 8, 12 or even more electrons and yielding diverse reduction products including carbon monoxide (CO), formic acid (HCOOH), methane (CH4), ethylene (C2H4), and many others depending on the nature of the electrocatalysts or photocatalysts as well as the actual experimental conditions.27, 28 As a result, electrocatalytic or photocatalytic CO2 reduction is generally suffered from very limited efficiency and poor selectivity.

Electrocatalytic carbon dioxide reduction: from fundamental principles to catalyst design
=========================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Xiaolong Zhang a, Si-Xuan Guo a b, Karl A.Gandionco a b, [Alan M.Bond a b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/author/7202183777/alan-m-bond), Jie Zhang a b

Abstract
--------

Electrocatalytic reduction of [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CO 2) is one of the most promising technologies available to mitigate environmental problems introduced by the continuous increase in its concentration that has occurred since the industrial revolution. This technology also is attractive from the viewpoint that it can be based on carbon-neutral energy by converting CO 2 into fuels and value-added chemicals using solar or other forms of renewable energy. [In](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/indium "Learn more about In from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") this review, fundamental concepts related to [electrocatalysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrocatalysis "Learn more about electrocatalysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the electrical double layer, physical and chemical properties of CO 2, the interaction of CO 2 with electrode surfaces, and the sources of carbon and proton that underpin this technology are provided. Insights into recent mechanisms proposed for the electrocatalytic reduction of CO 2 also are provided along with a survey of progress in the rational design of new [electrocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrocatalysts "Learn more about electrocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Discussion on achievements in the field is focused on the variation in products formed by different [electrocatalysts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalyst "Learn more about electrocatalysts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the design strategies that have been introduced to alter the electronic and geometric properties of the electrocatalysts. Finally, recommendations for achieving further advances in electrocatalytic reduction of CO 2 in a systematic manner are presented.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about Carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CO 2) is an important carbon source needed to sustain Life on Earth. Historically, its concentration in the atmosphere has been regulated by photosynthetic organisms and geological phenomena. [In](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/indium "Learn more about In from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") Nature, about 203 gigatons of CO 2 are recycled each year [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib2)]. Until recently, Nature has been able to maintain a [carbon cycle](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/carbon-cycle "Learn more about carbon cycle from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") based on a delicate balance between the consumption and production when both involve natural processes. However, the consumption of [fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by human activity is currently producing over 30 additional gigatons of CO 2 per year [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib3)] that cannot be accommodated in the natural carbon cycle. As a result, the CO 2 concentration in the atmosphere has increased dramatically in the last 100 years [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib4)], and the accompanying greenhouse effect has a major impact on climate change [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib5)]. Accordingly, it is vital to develop technologies that minimize CO 2 accumulation in the environment [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib6),[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib7)].

Producing an anthropogenic carbon cycle by converting CO 2 and H 2 O into fuels and chemicals using solar or other forms of renewable energy is an appealing strategy to remove excess CO 2 [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib8),[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib9)]. This strategy not only mitigates energy and environmental issues related to CO 2 emission, but also creates a profitable carbon economy [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib10),[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib11)]. [Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fig1) illustrates a simplified version of an anthropogenic carbon cycle. [In](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/indium "Learn more about In from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such a system, renewable energy is converted to chemical energy stored in CO 2-derived fuels and chemicals. The key to close this anthropogenic carbon cycle is through the reduction of CO 2 to useful chemicals [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib12)]. Techniques such as biological, thermochemical, photochemical, and electrochemical ones are being widely studied for CO 2 reduction. Among these techniques, the electrochemical conversion of CO 2 has several advantages, including mild reaction conditions, controllable reaction rates and product selectivity through the applied potential, and wide scalability due to modular electrolyzer designs. Electrocatalytic reduction of CO 2 coupled with intermittent [renewable energy sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/renewable-energy-source "Learn more about renewable energy sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a promising method for increasing the application of renewable energy in the fuel and chemical industries [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib13)].

![Image 12: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590049820300217-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590049820300217-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig.1. Illustration of a simplified anthropogenic [carbon cycle](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/carbon-cycle "Learn more about carbon cycle from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

2. Fundamentals of electrocatalytic CO 2 reduction
--------------------------------------------------

### 2.1. A brief introduction of the fundamentals of electrocatalysis

Electrocatalysis is a combination of [electrochemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrochemistry "Learn more about electrochemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and catalysis. Electrochemistry interconverts electrical and chemical energy, while catalysis increases the rate of a chemical reaction. Because [electrocatalysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalysis "Learn more about electrocatalysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") achieves chemical transformation at an electrode surface, it can be regarded as a special form of [heterogeneous catalysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/heterogeneous-catalysis "Learn more about heterogeneous catalysis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that involves interaction and electron exchange between reactants and an [electrocatalyst](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalyst "Learn more about electrocatalyst from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the latter often being the electrode or a component of the electrode [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib14)]. An overall electrocatalytic process consists of cathodic and anodic processes occurring in two compartments separated by a membrane to prevent the mixing of the cathodic and anodic reaction products, as illustrated in [Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fig2)a. During this process, a non-spontaneous [electron transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electron-transport "Learn more about electron transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reaction is driven by an external power supply, so that the electrical energy is converted to the chemical energy of the reaction products. The cathodic/anodic components of the process involve electron-conducting phases (electrodes) connected by an ion-conducting phase (electrolyte medium). However, as the cathode electrode/electrolyte interface is where electrocatalytic CO 2 reduction reactions take place, a detailed understanding of this interfacial region is needed.

![Image 13: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590049820300217-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590049820300217-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig.2. Schematic illustration of (a) a conventional electrocatalytic system and (b) the electrical double layer structure at the cathode, which is of primary concern with respect to CO 2 reduction. IHP and OHP are the inner and outer Helmholtz planes, respectively.

[In](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/indium "Learn more about In from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") electrochemistry related to CO 2 reduction, electrodes are under potentiostatic control and influenced by the negative charge on the surface of the electrode. This results in strong interactions between the ions/solvent molecules in solution and the electrode surface, and the formation of a region called the electrical double layer ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fig2)b) which consists of an array of charged species and/or oriented dipoles [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib16)]. The structure of the interface is typically divided into five distinct regions, namely the electrode surface, the inner Helmholtz plane (IHP), the outer Helmholtz plane (OHP), the diffuse layer, and the bulk solution [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib15)]. The closest layer to the electrode is the IHP, which may contain specifically adsorbed reactants, reaction intermediates, reaction products, solvent molecules, and solvated electrolyte ions. The OHP is comprised primarily of solvated ions with the opposite charge to that of the electrode, which are non-specifically adsorbed and interact with the electrode via long-range [electrostatic forces](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrostatic-force "Learn more about electrostatic forces from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The IHP and OHP form a compact layer. The diffuse layer, which is a three-dimensional region consisting of non-specifically adsorbed ions, extends from the OHP to the bulk solution. The Helmholtz layer and the diffuse layer form the electrical double layer. The total charge density of the electrical double layer, which includes the charges of both the compact and diffuse layers, equals that of the electrode, but with an opposite sign [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib16)]. The rates of electrode processes can be affected by the structure of the double layer.

The electrocatalytic CO 2 reduction reaction (CO 2 RR) is an inner-sphere process [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib17)], where the adsorption and bond rearrangement of the CO 2 molecule and reaction intermediates proceed within the IHP [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib18)]. When a CO 2 molecule approaches the electrode surface, interactions with both the electrolyte medium and the electrode come into play. Electrocatalytic processes are therefore influenced by factors such as the potential-dependent structure of the solvent, reaction-induced concentration gradients, potential-dependent behavior of electrolyte ions, strong interactions of electrolyte ions with the electrode surface and adsorbed species and their impact on the double layer structure, reaction activity and product selectivity.

### 2.2. Physical and chemical properties of CO 2

The CO 2 molecule is linear and centrosymmetric. The C![Image 14: double bond](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/shared-assets/55/entities/dbnd.gif)O bond length is 116.3 pm, which is markedly shorter than that of a C–O [single bond](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/single-bond "Learn more about single bond from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and also shorter than carbon-oxygen bonds in many other multiply bonded functional groups, such as the C–O bond in carbonyls [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib19)]. Although the C![Image 15: double bond](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/shared-assets/55/entities/dbnd.gif)O bonds in CO 2 are polar, the [dipole moment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/dipole-moment "Learn more about dipole moment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the molecule is zero because the two bond dipoles cancel each other due to the linear structure. The carbon center of the CO 2 molecule is electrophilic. The bonding electrons are more closely associated with oxygen than with carbon due to the difference in their electron negativities. CO 2 itself is a highly stable form of carbon with a C=O bond energy of 805 kJ mol-1 [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib20)]. The direct decomposition of CO 2 to CO and O 2 yields a large enthalpy change (Δ _H_ o) of 283.0 kJ mol-1 under standard conditions (1 atm, 298.15 K) [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib21)].(1)CO 2 (g) → CO (g)+1/2 O 2 (g); Δ _H_ o=283.0 kJ mol-1

The standard electrode potentials (_E_ o) for selected CO 2 RR half-reactions are provided in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#tbl1) [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib22),[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib23)]. However, the sluggish kinetics of the CO 2 RR means that a much more negative potential is required to drive the reactions at an appreciable rate than implied by the _E_ o value. Significantly, the [activation energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/reaction-activation-energy "Learn more about activation energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the CO 2 molecule to form the CO 2•−[radical anion](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/radical-anion "Learn more about radical anion from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which is often the first elementary [electron transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electron-transfer "Learn more about electron transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") step in a CO 2 RR, only proceeds at a very negative applied potential (−1.9 V vs. the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) at a non-catalytic surface) [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib24)]. Accordingly, in the development of electrocatalysts for the CO 2 RR, it is vital to lower the kinetic energy barriers and improve energy efficiency.

Table 1. Selected _E_ o values for the CO 2 RR (V vs. SHE) at 1.0 atm and 25°C.

| CO 2 RR half-reactions | _E_ o (V vs. SHE) |
| --- | --- |
| CO 2(g)+ 2H+(aq)+ 2e- → CO(g)+ H 2 O(l) | −0.106 |
| CO 2(g)+ 2H+(aq)+ 2e- → HCOOH(l) | −0.199 |
| CO 2(g)+ 6H+(aq)+ 6e- → CH 3 OH(l)+ H 2 O(l) | 0.030 |
| CO 2(g)+ 8H+(aq)+ 8e- → CH 4(g)+ 2H 2 O(l) | 0.169 |
| 2CO 2(g)+ 12H+(aq)+ 12e- → CH 2 CH 2(g)+ 4H 2 O(l) | 0.064 |
| 2CO 2(g)+ 12H+(aq)+ 12e- → CH 3 CH 2 OH(l)+ 3H 2 O(l) | 0.09 |

### 2.3. Interaction of CO 2 molecules with the electrode surface

Under CO 2 RR conditions, when a CO 2 molecule adsorbs onto an electrode surface, it binds to the surface through [chemisorption](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/chemisorption "Learn more about chemisorption from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which involves electron redistribution and the formation of a chemical bond. Discussion on how CO 2 binds to a catalyst surface is helpful in understanding the [chemisorption](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/chemisorption "Learn more about chemisorption from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") process, which often is the first elementary step of the CO 2 RR. [Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fig3)a shows the [molecular orbital](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/molecular-orbital "Learn more about molecular orbital from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") diagram for CO 2. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the [lowest unoccupied molecular orbital](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/lowest-unoccupied-molecular-orbital "Learn more about lowest unoccupied molecular orbital from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LUMO) of CO 2 are 1π g and 2π u, respectively [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib25)]. Both the relative order as well as the absolute HOMO and LUMO energy levels greatly influence the adsorbate-surface chemistry. Furthermore, chemisorption is highly directional, like all chemical bonds. Consequently, chemisorbed adsorbates (chemisorbates) adhere to specific sites, and this binding interaction strongly depends on their orientation with respect to the surface.

![Image 16: Fig. 3](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590049820300217-gr3.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2590049820300217-gr3.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig.3. (a) Energy levels for [molecular orbitals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/molecular-orbital "Learn more about molecular orbitals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of CO 2. Reproduced with permission [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib26)]. Copyright Andrew R. Barron. (b) Possible coordination modes of CO 2 on a metal surface.

The orientation of the HOMO and LUMO with regard to the surface determines the nature of the bonding. Both 1π g and 2π u orbitals distribute symmetrically along the molecular axis. The fully occupied 1π g orbital strongly interacts with the electronic states of a metallic electrode. Effectively, the electron density of the 1π g orbital is donated to the electrode resulting in new hybrid electronic states. The 2π u orbital accepts electrons from the electrode and forms new hybrid electronic states that are primarily localized around the CO 2 molecule. The chemisorbed CO 2 molecule has a bent configuration [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib27)]. Three possible configuration types for chemisorbed CO 2 molecule on a metal surface have been proposed, as shown in [Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fig3)b. However, the C s configuration can be excluded because no asymmetric stretching loss in the specular direction has been observed [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib27)]. The C end down in the C 2v configuration is preferred for the CO and hydrocarbon-producing electrocatalysts, based on the fact that the overlap of both 1π g and 2π u orbitals with the metal states is most favored by a linear geometry, and the carbon center is electrophilic. [In](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/indium "Learn more about In from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") contrast, the O end down C 2v configuration is adopted for formate-producing electrocatalysts. As the 1π g orbital of CO 2 molecule is non-bonding, modification of this orbital does not have a strong influence on the intramolecular C![Image 17: double bond](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/shared-assets/55/entities/dbnd.gif)O bonds. However, the 2π u orbital is antibonding, hence, the increased occupation of this orbital leads to a weakening of the C![Image 18: double bond](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/shared-assets/55/entities/dbnd.gif)O bonds and thus higher reactivity of the CO 2 molecule [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib28)]. The above overview provides a simplified illustration of how a catalyst can decrease the [activation energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/activation-energy "Learn more about activation energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") barrier for the CO 2 RR. The actual CO 2 RR mechanism is far more complicated, as discussed later.

### 2.4. Performance indicators for electrocatalytic CO 2 reduction systems

Before proceeding to the consideration of actual data obtained experimentally, it is useful to introduce the performance indicators that are widely used to compare CO 2 RR systems. The evaluation of the performance of a CO 2 RR system is much more complicated than the [hydrogen evolution reaction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/hydrogen-evolution "Learn more about hydrogen evolution reaction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HER) or the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) due to the inherently greater complexity arising from the co-existence of multiple proton-coupled electron transfer reaction pathways that can lead to numerous products. The competing hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) also can consume a considerable amount of the input charge. High selectivity for the desired product is most advantageous to avoid wasting electricity on unwanted products and also to facilitate product separation. Normally, product selectivity is evaluated by measuring the product faradaic efficiency (FE) [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#bib29)], which is calculated via Equation [(2)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590049820300217#fd2) where _n_ is the number of electrons transferred per mole of product, _F_ is the Faraday constant, _N_ is the number of moles of the product obtained by electrolysis at a certain potential, and _Q_ is the total charge consumed by the electrolysis.(2)FE=_nFN_/_Q_×100%

enriched_points.expanded_content: Utilize high temperatures to drive reactions converting CO₂ into fuels or chemicals, often involving metal oxides as catalysts to facilitate the process efficiently.
Leverage sunlight to energize chemical reactions that convert CO₂ into useful products, utilizing photocatalysts to harness solar energy effectively for carbon reduction.
Involve electron transfer reactions facilitated by electrodes, converting CO₂ into various products using electrical energy, often resulting in higher selectivity and mild reaction conditions.
Complex processes that involve the transfer of multiple electrons, enabling the conversion of CO₂ into a wide range of products, depending on the conditions and catalysts used.
CO₂ reduction yields diverse products including carbon monoxide, formic acid, and hydrocarbons, influenced by the reaction pathway, catalyst, and conditions that dictate selectivity.
